NJ School Data Almanac
North Star Academy Charter School
ESSEX COUNTY  ·  CHARTER DISTRICT  ·  no DFG  ·  6,812 STUDENTS (2026)

Ten things the data says about North Star Academy Charter School. Every figure is computed from New Jersey Department of Education data via the njschooldata package — nothing is estimated. Comparisons use District Factor Group (DFG) peers (616 districts).

No. 1 ENROLLMENT

North Star Doubled Its Enrollment Since 2015, Adding 3,409 Students to Reach 6,812

The Newark charter network grew from 3,403 students in 2015 to a record 6,812 in 2026, a 100.2% expansion.

Few public schools in New Jersey have grown the way North Star Academy Charter School has. In 2015 the Newark charter network enrolled 3,403 students. By 2026 it enrolled 6,812 — an increase of 3,409 students, or 100.2%, and the largest enrollment the network has ever recorded.

The growth was steady rather than sudden. North Star added roughly 500 to 650 students a year through the late 2010s, climbing 17.2% in 2016 and 12.3% in 2020 alone. Even through the pandemic the network kept expanding, gaining 1,016 students between 2019 and 2022 — a 19.3% jump during years when many traditional districts statewide were shrinking.

Since 2022 the pace has cooled to low single digits, with the most recent year adding 98 students, a 1.5% gain. That slowdown raises a question the enrollment numbers alone cannot answer: is North Star approaching the ceiling its charter and facilities allow, or simply pausing before another expansion?

No. 2 COLLEGE READINESS

Nearly Half of North Star Students Pass an AP or IB Exam — Double the State Rate

In 2024, 45.9% of North Star students scored 3+ on an AP exam or 4+ on an IB exam, against a statewide rate of 23.0%.

At a school where 86.1% of students qualify for free or reduced-price lunch, the advanced-coursework numbers stand out. In 2024, 45.9% of North Star students earned a passing score — a 3 or higher on an Advanced Placement exam, or a 4 or higher on an International Baccalaureate exam. The statewide figure that year was 23.0%, meaning North Star’s pass rate was roughly double the state average.

The result is not a one-year spike. North Star’s share of students passing an AP or IB exam topped the state every year in the data, from 43.9% in 2018 (state 20.5%) to a high of 54.6% in 2020. Participation is broad as well: 82% of students took AP or IB coursework in 2024, and 80.7% sat for an exam.

That North Star clears the state bar by such a wide margin in a high-poverty student body is the kind of result families and authorizers watch closely. The open question is whether passing rates can hold as the network’s enrollment keeps climbing and its cohorts grow larger.

No. 3 GRADUATION

North Star’s 88.3% Graduation Rate Sits in the 13th Percentile Among Its 616 Peers

The network graduated 88.3% of its 2025 cohort, below the 94.7% median for the 616 districts it is compared against.

North Star’s college-prep results are strong, but its four-year graduation rate tells a more complicated story. The network graduated 88.3% of its 2025 senior cohort of 350 students. Against the 616 districts it is benchmarked with, that placed North Star in the 13th percentile — well below the peer median of 94.7%.

The rate has been stable rather than rising. Over six cohorts it ranged from a low of 81.8% in 2022 to the current 88.3%, never reaching the 90s. Meanwhile the network’s tested-achievement metrics, from AP pass rates to NJGPA English, run at or above state levels — a contrast that suggests the gap is less about academic preparation than about getting every enrolled senior across the finish line on time.

With cohorts growing — from 258 graduates in 2020 to 350 in 2025 — the question is whether North Star can lift its on-time completion rate toward its peers while its senior classes keep expanding.

No. 4 ATTENDANCE

North Star’s Chronic Absenteeism Hit 21.7% in 2024 — Nearly Double Its Peers’ 11.4%

More than one in five North Star students were chronically absent in 2024, against a peer median of 11.4%.

In 2024, 21.7% of North Star students were chronically absent — missing 10% or more of the school year. That rate was nearly double the 11.4% median among the network’s peer districts.

The trend traces a familiar pandemic arc. Chronic absenteeism stood at 16.6% in 2019, surged to 27.6% in 2022 as schools navigated the return from remote learning, then eased to 24.2% in 2023 and 21.7% in 2024. But even after two years of improvement, the 2024 rate remained above where it sat before the pandemic — and far above the peer benchmark.

Chronic absence is one of the strongest early warning signs for the on-time graduation gap North Star is also navigating. Whether the network can pull attendance back below its pre-COVID 16.6% — and closer to its peers — is among the clearest tests in the data.

No. 5 DEMOGRAPHICS

North Star’s Black Enrollment Share Fell From 89% to 81% as Hispanic Share Quadrupled Since 2015

Between 2015 and 2026 the network’s Hispanic share climbed from 3.4% to 15.9% while its Black share declined from 89.1% to 80.5%.

North Star remains a predominantly Black school, but its student mix has shifted measurably over a decade. In 2015, 89.1% of students were Black, 3.4% were Hispanic, and 5% were white. By 2026, the Black share had fallen to 80.5%, the Hispanic share had climbed to 15.9%, and the white share had dropped to 1.3%.

The Hispanic share saw the largest movement of any group — a gain of 12.5 percentage points — while the Black share fell 8.7 points and the white share fell 3.7 points. The change tracks with broader demographic shifts in Newark, where the network draws nearly all of its students.

The shift coincides with another change at North Star: students classified as multilingual learners. As the Hispanic share grew, the question becomes how the network’s instruction and supports keep pace with a student body that looks different than it did when the school was half its current size.

No. 6 DEMOGRAPHICS

North Star’s Multilingual Learner Count Grew From Zero to 327 in a Decade

Students classified as limited English proficient rose from none in 2015 to 327, or 4.8% of enrollment, in 2026.

When North Star enrolled 3,403 students in 2015, none were classified as limited English proficient. By 2026, the network counted 327 such students, or 4.8% of its enrollment — a population it built from scratch as it doubled in size.

The growth has accelerated recently. The multilingual learner share sat near 1.2% to 1.6% through the late 2010s, then climbed each year from 1.9% in 2023 to 2.8% in 2024, 3.8% in 2025, and 4.8% in 2026. The rise mirrors the network’s growing Hispanic enrollment over the same period.

The trend points to a population that barely existed at North Star a decade ago and is now its fastest-growing subgroup. The data raises a question it cannot resolve: how a network built around a college-prep model adapts its supports as its share of multilingual learners keeps climbing.

No. 7 EQUITY

North Star Girls Graduated at 93.3% in 2025; Boys at 82.8%, a 10-Point Gap

Among 2025 graduates, female students completed at 93.3% while male students completed at 82.8%.

North Star’s overall 88.3% graduation rate masks a sharp split by gender. Among the network’s 2025 cohort, 93.3% of female students graduated on time — above the peer median — while 82.8% of male students did, a gap of more than 10 percentage points.

Other subgroup rates clustered closer to the overall figure: Black students graduated at 88.8%, students who are economically disadvantaged at 88.5%, and Hispanic students at 85.7%. Students with disabilities graduated at 81.6%, the lowest reported rate among the network’s subgroups, from a cohort of 49.

The pattern — girls outpacing boys, students with disabilities trailing — is common across New Jersey, but the size of North Star’s gender gap stands out. The data leaves open why the network’s young men complete at a rate more than 10 points below their female classmates.

No. 8 ACHIEVEMENT

North Star Tests 86.9% Proficient in NJGPA English But 62.3% in Math

On the 2025 NJGPA, 86.9% of North Star students met the English bar while 62.3% met the math bar — a 24-point gap.

North Star’s high-school students cleared the state’s English proficiency bar at a high rate in 2025: 86.9% scored proficient or above on the NJGPA English exam, up from 84.6% in 2023. Math told a different story, with 62.3% proficient or above — a gap of roughly 24 percentage points between the two subjects.

The math result has held remarkably flat. Across three years the share proficient moved only narrowly, from 62.2% in 2023 to 64.5% in 2024 to 62.3% in 2025, even as English climbed. The divide between strong English performance and stuck math results is the central tension in the network’s testing data.

For a network that sends nearly half its students to passing AP or IB scores, the persistent math gap on the state graduation assessment is a puzzle the numbers surface but do not explain.

No. 9 COLLEGE READINESS

Every North Star Student Takes the SAT — While Statewide Participation Sits Near 63%

North Star reported 100% SAT participation every year from 2018 to 2024, against a state rate that fell as low as 45.4%.

North Star has held SAT participation at 100% in every year on record, from 2018 through 2024. Over the same stretch, statewide participation slid from 72.2% in 2018 to 45.4% in 2021 during the pandemic, recovering only to 62.7% by 2024.

Universal testing is a deliberate signal of the network’s college-going model, ensuring every senior leaves with a college-entrance score regardless of family resources — at a school where more than 86% of students qualify for free or reduced-price lunch. It also pairs with North Star’s broad AP and IB participation to keep its students inside the college-admissions pipeline.

With participation already at the ceiling, the next question the data invites is what those scores show — a figure this dataset records the rate of, but not the result of.
